structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health record (EHR) system from a single hospital ED, covering 24 months of patient encounters (approximately 100,000 records). Raw signals include: patient check-in timestamp, triage level (1–5), staffing level at time of check-in, bed availability, number of patients in waiting room, time of day, day of week, holiday flag, and actual time to clinician contact (the label).
structured_choices.label_definition: Time (in minutes) from patient check-in completion to first clinician contact. Defined consistently across all encounters. Outliers (e.g., patients who left without being seen, transfers, administrative holds) are excluded from the dataset prior to any modeling. Final dataset size: 92,000 encounters after exclusion.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by day-type category. The 24-month period is divided into three contiguous windows: months 1–16 (training, n≈61,000), months 17–20 (validation, n≈15,000), months 21–24 (test, n≈16,000). Within each window, encounters are further stratified by day type (weekday, weekend, holiday) to ensure balanced representation in train/val/test. This temporal ordering prevents label leakage (future information does not inform past predictions) and reflects realistic deployment: the model is trained on historical data and evaluated on temporally subsequent data. Stratification ensures that weekend and holiday subgroups are represented proportionally in all three splits, enabling separate performance assessment.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for numeric features, OneHotEncoder for categorical features) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–16). The fitted transformers are then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Imputation of missing values (expected in staffing level and bed availability fields, ~3% of records) is performed using median imputation fit on training data only. This prevents data leakage.
structured_choices.features: Raw features ingested: check-in hour (0–23), day of week (0–6 encoded), holiday flag (binary), triage level (ordinal, 1–5), staffing level (integer count at check-in time), bed availability (integer count), patients waiting (integer count at check-in). Engineered temporal features: hour-of-day sine/cosine transformation (cyclic encoding for 24-hour periodicity), day-of-week sine/cosine transformation (7-day periodicity), rolling 6-hour mean of incoming patient count (lag-free, computed from training data statistics only and applied as a scalar feature). All numeric features are standardized to mean 0, SD 1 post-imputation. Triage level is ordinally encoded (no one-hot encoding, as it is ordinal). No feature is constructed from the label; no future information (arrival of subsequent patients, actual downstream interventions) is included.
structured_choices.model: Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with the following architecture: input layer accepting sequences of length 12 (patient arrivals in the 12 hours preceding check-in, represented as 12 time steps with 7 features per step: hour, day-of-week, holiday, triage, staffing, beds, waiting). Two dilated convolutional blocks (64 filters, kernel size 3, dilation rate 1 then 2), batch normalization, ReLU activation, dropout (0.2 per block). A global average pooling layer, followed by two fully connected layers (128, 64 units, ReLU, dropout 0.3), and a linear output layer predicting wait time in minutes. Trained with mean squared error (MSE) loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001, default betas), batch size 32, early stopping on validation loss with patience 10 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Standard linear regression (OLS) with the same feature set (all 7 engineered features, no sequencing). The baseline receives identical preprocessing (standardization fit on training data) and identical tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (L2, alpha in {0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}) on the validation set. The baseline is evaluated on the same train/val/test split and under the same stratification. This ensures fair comparison: the TCN's advantage cannot be attributed to better tuning or richer features, but rather to its capacity to model temporal dependencies. Both models are given the same computational budget and cross-validation setup.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in minutes, computed separately for three subgroups: (1) weekday encounters, (2) weekend encounters, (3) holiday encounters. The primary claim is that TCN MAE on the combined test set is significantly lower than linear regression MAE (paired t-test, α=0.05, two-tailed, on per-encounter prediction errors). Secondary reporting: MAE for each day-type subgroup independently. MAE is chosen because stakeholders care about typical prediction error magnitude; a prediction 10 minutes off is operationally meaningful regardless of whether true wait time is 30 or 60 minutes. MAE is also robust to outliers in a way that MSE is not, and is directly interpretable to ED operations staff.
secondary_metrics: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on test set — captures larger prediction errors more heavily; useful if high outlier errors are operationally costly.
Median Absolute Error (MedAE) — robustness check for whether MAE is driven by a small number of large errors.
95th percentile absolute error — identifies worst-case prediction performance, relevant for SLA or resource planning.
Calibration curve (predicted vs. actual wait time quantiles) — assesses whether the model's confidence is well-calibrated; important if uncertainty estimates will be used downstream.
Separate MAE for each triage level (1–5) — ensures the model does not systematically fail on high-acuity (triage 1–2) or low-acuity (triage 4–5) patients.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for both TCN and linear regression is performed on the validation set (months 17–20). Grid search over TCN hyperparameters (number of blocks in {1, 2, 3}, filter counts in {32, 64, 128}, dropout in {0.1, 0.2, 0.3}) is conducted by training each configuration on the training set and evaluating MAE on the validation set. The configuration with lowest validation MAE is retained. Linear regression hyperparameter search is performed identically: fit on training data for each alpha value, evaluate on validation set, retain the alpha with lowest validation MAE. The test set (months 21–24) is not touched during this process and is used only for final reporting.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held entirely separate from model development and is used only once, at the end, for final evaluation. Specifically: (1) no data from months 21–24 is visible during feature engineering, preprocessing fit, or hyperparameter tuning; (2) both the TCN and baseline models are finalized on the basis of training and validation data only; (3) the test set is loaded, preprocessed using transformers fit on training data, and both models make predictions on it; (4) metrics are computed and reported on the full test set and on subgroups (weekday, weekend, holiday). No further tuning or model selection is performed on test data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: patient case severity and mix differ on weekends vs. weekdays. Control: (a) stratified train/val/test split ensures each day-type category is represented proportionally in all three splits; (b) triage level is included as an explicit feature in both models, allowing the models to adjust predictions based on acuity; (c) separate MAE reporting for each day-type subgroup (weekday, weekend, holiday) reveals whether the TCN advantage is consistent across day types or driven by a single subgroup; if TCN outperforms on weekdays but underperforms on weekends, this suggests a day-specific phenomenon, not a universally superior method. Known confound: staffing levels and resource availability affect wait times. Control: (a) staffing level and bed availability are included as explicit features, allowing both models to condition on resource constraints; (b) if staffing levels are highly correlated with day type (e.g., lower staffing on weekends), the stratified split ensures this correlation is preserved in train/val/test; (c) during final reporting, we compute MAE conditional on low vs. high staffing to assess whether model advantage persists across resource regimes. Scope: This experiment establishes whether TCNs can achieve lower prediction error than linear regression on this dataset under the specified conditions. It does not establish causality (e.g., whether lower wait times are actually achieved by deploying the TCN for operational decisions), nor does it guarantee generalization to other hospitals or EDs with different patient populations, staffing models, or infrastructure.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: TCN vs. Linear Regression for ED Wait Time Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Preprocessing

We will source 24 months of operational data from the hospital ED's EHR system. The raw dataset includes approximately 100,000 patient encounters; after removing incomplete records, patients who left without being seen, and administrative outliers, the usable dataset contains 92,000 encounters.

For each encounter, we extract: check-in timestamp (parsed into hour 0–23 and day-of-week 0–6), holiday flag (binary), triage level (1–5, ordinal), staffing level at check-in (integer), bed availability (integer), and the current waiting room count. The label is time-to-clinician-contact in minutes, defined as the interval from check-in completion to first documented clinician contact. This definition is applied consistently across all records.

We identify and address three data quality issues: (a) missing staffing levels and bed counts (~3% of records) are imputed using median values computed on training data only; (b) physiologically implausible wait times (e.g., <1 minute or >480 minutes) are reviewed; values <1 minute are recoded as 1 minute (system precision limit), and values >480 minutes are excluded (n=180) as likely data entry errors or extraordinary circumstances; (c) temporal gaps (e.g., system downtime) are flagged and excluded from analysis.

### 2. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

We construct engineered features to capture temporal structure:

- **Cyclic encoding:** Hour-of-day (0–23) and day-of-week (0–6) are transformed using sine/cosine pairs to preserve circularity.
- **Temporal context:** A rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed from training data statistics and applied as a scalar feature, reflecting the local patient inflow rate at check-in time.
- **Raw features:** Triage level (ordinal, not one-hot encoded), staffing level, bed availability, and waiting room count are included as-is.

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit exclusively on the training set. This prevents information leakage into the validation and test sets. No feature is constructed from the label or from future information.

### 3. Data Splitting Strategy

The 24-month period is divided chronologically into three contiguous windows:

- **Training (Months 1–16):** 61,000 encounters
- **Validation (Months 17–20):** 15,000 encounters
- **Test (Months 21–24):** 16,000 encounters

Within each window, encounters are stratified by day type (weekday, weekend, holiday) to ensure balanced representation. This temporal split prevents label leakage (the model cannot train on data from months 17–24 when predicting month 17–24 outcomes) and reflects realistic deployment: the model is trained on historical data and tested on subsequent held-out data. Stratification ensures that weekend and holiday patterns—the focus of the hypothesis—are represented proportionally across splits, enabling reliable subgroup analysis.

### 4. Model and Baseline Definition

**Proposed Model (TCN):** A temporal convolutional network designed to capture temporal dependencies in patient arrivals. The architecture includes:

- Input sequences of length 12 (the 12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in), with 7 features per time step.
- Two dilated convolutional blocks (64 filters, kernel size 3, dilation rates 1 and 2) with batch normalization, ReLU activation, and dropout (0.2).
- Global average pooling, followed by two fully connected layers (128 and 64 units, ReLU, dropout 0.3).
- A linear output layer predicting wait time in minutes.
- Trained with MSE loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001), batch size 32, and early stopping on validation loss (patience 10 epochs).

**Baseline (Linear Regression):** Standard ordinary least squares (OLS) with L2 regularization, trained on the same feature set (all 7 engineered features, no sequencing). The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search over regularization strength (alpha in {0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}) using the validation set.

Both models use identical preprocessing, train/val/test splits, and evaluation protocol. This ensures that any performance difference is attributable to the TCN's temporal modeling capacity, not to better tuning or feature richness.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using grid search. For the TCN: number of convolutional blocks in {1, 2, 3}, filter counts in {32, 64, 128}, and dropout rates in {0.1, 0.2, 0.3}. For linear regression: alpha in {0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}. The configuration achieving the lowest Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on validation data is retained. The test set remains untouched throughout development and is used only for final evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in minutes, computed on the test set. Reported separately for weekday, weekend, and holiday encounters. We will conduct a paired t-test (α=0.05, two-tailed) on per-encounter absolute prediction errors to determine statistical significance. MAE is chosen because it directly reflects prediction error magnitude in operationally meaningful units (minutes); a 10-minute prediction error has consistent interpretability whether the true wait time is 30 or 60 minutes.

**Secondary Metrics:**

- Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): captures tail behavior and larger prediction errors.
- Median Absolute Error (MedAE): robustness check; identifies whether MAE is driven by outliers.
- 95th percentile absolute error: worst-case performance, relevant for resource planning and SLAs.
- Calibration analysis: predicted vs. actual wait time quantiles, assessing whether the model's uncertainty estimates are well-calibrated.
- Stratified MAE by triage level (1–5): ensures no systematic failure on high-acuity or low-acuity patients.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

**Patient case severity and mix:** Triage level is included as an explicit feature in both models, allowing prediction to adjust based on acuity. Stratified splitting ensures weekend and holiday subgroups are represented in all splits. Separate MAE reporting by day type reveals whether the TCN advantage is uniform or subgroup-specific.

**Staffing and resource availability:** Staffing level and bed availability are included as explicit features. Additional analysis computes MAE conditional on low vs. high staffing regimes to assess robustness.

**Scope:** This experiment tests whether TCNs achieve lower prediction error than linear regression on this specific dataset under the stated conditions. It does not establish whether operational deployment of the TCN would reduce actual wait times, nor does it guarantee generalization to other hospitals or EDs with different patient populations or infrastructure. Claims are limited to prediction accuracy on this dataset.
